instruction: How do I implement typed errors in TypeScript? Show the Result type pattern, typed error unions, and error boundaries.
output:
<think>
TypeScript's error handling has a fundamental problem: throw/catch is untyped. You can throw anything, and catch gives you `unknown`. This leads to defensive instanceof checks everywhere.

Better patterns:
1. Result type (discriminated union): No exceptions, errors are part of the return type.
2. Typed error unions: Define specific error types and return them in the Result.
3. Never type for exhaustive checking: TypeScript's `never` type ensures you handle all error variants.

The Result pattern is gaining popularity because: errors are visible in the type signature, no try/catch needed, compiler enforces handling, works with discriminated unions for exhaustive matching.

The tradeoff: more verbose than throw/catch, doesn't work with async/await naturally (need to wrap), ecosystem expects exceptions. I'll show both the Result pattern and typed error unions with exhaustive handling.
</think>

```typescript
// Result type (discriminated union)
type Result<T, E = Error> =
  | { ok: true; value: T }
  | { ok: false; error: E };

function Ok<T>(value: T): Result<T, never> {
  return { ok: true, value };
}

function Err<E>(error: E): Result<never, E> {
  return { ok: false, error };
}

// Typed error union
type UserError =
  | { type: 'NOT_FOUND'; userId: string }
  | { type: 'VALIDATION'; field: string; message: string }
  | { type: 'DUPLICATE'; email: string }
  | { type: 'UNAUTHORIZED'; requiredRole: string };

interface User {
  id: string;
  email: string;
  name: string;
}

const users = new Map<string, User>();

function createUser(email: string, name: string): Result<User, UserError> {
  if (!email.includes('@')) {
    return Err({ type: 'VALIDATION', field: 'email', message: 'invalid format' });
  }
  if (name.length < 2) {
    return Err({ type: 'VALIDATION', field: 'name', message: 'too short' });
  }
  if (users.has(email)) {
    return Err({ type: 'DUPLICATE', email });
  }
  const user: User = { id: `usr_${Date.now()}`, email, name };
  users.set(email, user);
  return Ok(user);
}

// Exhaustive error handling with never
function assertNever(value: never): never {
  throw new Error(`Unexpected value: ${JSON.stringify(value)}`);
}

function handleUserError(error: UserError): string {
  switch (error.type) {
    case 'NOT_FOUND': return `User ${error.userId} not found`;
    case 'VALIDATION': return `Invalid ${error.field}: ${error.message}`;
    case 'DUPLICATE': return `Email ${error.email} already registered`;
    case 'UNAUTHORIZED': return `Requires role: ${error.requiredRole}`;
    default: return assertNever(error);
  }
}

// Result utility functions
function map<T, U, E>(result: Result<T, E>, fn: (val: T) => U): Result<U, E> {
  return result.ok ? Ok(fn(result.value)) : result;
}

function flatMap<T, U, E>(result: Result<T, E>, fn: (val: T) => Result<U, E>): Result<U, E> {
  return result.ok ? fn(result.value) : result;
}

function unwrapOr<T, E>(result: Result<T, E>, defaultValue: T): T {
  return result.ok ? result.value : defaultValue;
}

// Wrapping exception-throwing code
function tryCatch<T>(fn: () => T): Result<T, Error> {
  try {
    return Ok(fn());
  } catch (e) {
    return Err(e instanceof Error ? e : new Error(String(e)));
  }
}

// Async Result
type AsyncResult<T, E = Error> = Promise<Result<T, E>>;

async function fetchUser(id: string): AsyncResult<User, UserError> {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
    if (res.status === 404) return Err({ type: 'NOT_FOUND', userId: id });
    if (res.status === 403) return Err({ type: 'UNAUTHORIZED', requiredRole: 'admin' });
    return Ok(await res.json() as User);
  } catch {
    return Err({ type: 'NOT_FOUND', userId: id });
  }
}

// Usage with full type narrowing
async function main() {
  const result = createUser('alice@example.com', 'Alice');
  if (result.ok) {
    console.log(`Created: ${result.value.name}`);
  } else {
    console.log(`Error: ${handleUserError(result.error)}`);
  }
}
```

instruction: Show TypeScript patterns for React error boundaries with proper typing, fallback UI, and error recovery mechanisms.
output:
React error boundaries are class components that catch JavaScript errors in their child component tree. Key points: - Must be class components (hooks can't catch render errors yet) - Catch errors in rendering, lifecycle methods, constructors - DON'T catch: event handlers, async code, SSR, errors in the boundary itself - Need both getDerivedStateFromError (update state) and componentDidCatch (side effects) For TypeScript typing: Props need children and fallback, state needs hasError and error, generic error boundary accepts a fallback render prop, reset mechanism for recovery. I'll show a production-quality error boundary with typed props/state, fallback render prop, reset/retry functionality, error reporting, and nested boundaries for granular handling. ```typescript import React, { Component, ReactNode, ErrorInfo, useCallback, useState } from 'react'; // Typed Error Boundary interface ErrorBoundaryProps { children: ReactNode; fallback: ReactNode | ((error: Error, reset: () => void) => ReactNode); onError?: (error: Error, errorInfo: ErrorInfo) => void; resetKeys?: unknown[]; } interface ErrorBoundaryState { hasError: boolean; error: Error | null; } class ErrorBoundary extends Component { constructor(props: ErrorBoundaryProps) { super(props); this.state = { hasError: false, error: null }; } static getDerivedStateFromError(error: Error): ErrorBoundaryState { return { hasError: true, error }; } componentDidCatch(error: Error, errorInfo: ErrorInfo): void { this.props.onError?.(error, errorInfo); } componentDidUpdate(prevProps: ErrorBoundaryProps): void { if (this.state.hasError && this.props.resetKeys) { const changed = this.props.resetKeys.some( (key, i) => key !== prevProps.resetKeys?.[i] ); if (changed) this.reset(); } } reset = (): void => { this.setState({ hasError: false, error: null }); }; render(): ReactNode { if (this.state.hasError && this.state.error) { const { fallback } = this.props; if (typeof fallback === 'function') { return fallback(this.state.error, this.reset); } return fallback; } return this.props.children; } } // Fallback components function FullPageError({ error, reset }: { error: Error; reset: () => void }) { return (

Something went wrong

{error.message}
); } function InlineError({ error, reset }: { error: Error; reset: () => void }) { return (

Failed to load: {error.message}

); } // Hook to push async errors into error boundaries function useAsyncError() { const [, setError] = useState(); return useCallback((error: Error) => { setError(() => { throw error; }); }, []); } // Nested boundaries for granular error handling function App() { const [retryKey, setRetryKey] = useState(0); return ( } onError={(error, info) => { fetch('/api/errors', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ message: error.message, stack: info.componentStack }), }).catch(() => {}); }} >
My App
} resetKeys={[retryKey]} > } resetKeys={[retryKey]} >
); } function Sidebar() { return
Sidebar
; } function MainContent() { return
Content
; } export { ErrorBoundary, useAsyncError }; ```

instruction: Show how to implement a type-safe Result type in TypeScript with chaining, async support, and collection utilities.
output:
<think>
Building a production-quality Result type in TypeScript requires: discriminated union with ok field, method chaining via utility functions, async/await support, good type narrowing, and utilities for arrays of Results.

I'll go with plain discriminated union type + utility namespace. This gives best type narrowing. For async, I'll create helpers that chain correctly with Promise<Result>.

Key utilities needed: map, flatMap, mapError, unwrapOr, fromThrowable, all (collect), partition (split successes/failures).
</think>

```typescript
export type Result<T, E> =
  | { readonly ok: true; readonly value: T }
  | { readonly ok: false; readonly error: E };

export const Ok = <T>(value: T): Result<T, never> => ({ ok: true, value });
export const Err = <E>(error: E): Result<never, E> => ({ ok: false, error });

export namespace Result {
  export function map<T, U, E>(r: Result<T, E>, fn: (v: T) => U): Result<U, E> {
    return r.ok ? Ok(fn(r.value)) : r;
  }

  export function flatMap<T, U, E>(r: Result<T, E>, fn: (v: T) => Result<U, E>): Result<U, E> {
    return r.ok ? fn(r.value) : r;
  }

  export function mapError<T, E, F>(r: Result<T, E>, fn: (e: E) => F): Result<T, F> {
    return r.ok ? r : Err(fn(r.error));
  }

  export function unwrapOr<T, E>(r: Result<T, E>, def: T): T {
    return r.ok ? r.value : def;
  }

  export function fromThrowable<T>(fn: () => T): Result<T, Error> {
    try { return Ok(fn()); }
    catch (e) { return Err(e instanceof Error ? e : new Error(String(e))); }
  }

  export function all<T, E>(results: Result<T, E>[]): Result<T[], E> {
    const values: T[] = [];
    for (const r of results) {
      if (!r.ok) return r;
      values.push(r.value);
    }
    return Ok(values);
  }

  export function partition<T, E>(results: Result<T, E>[]): [T[], E[]] {
    const oks: T[] = [], errs: E[] = [];
    for (const r of results) {
      if (r.ok) oks.push(r.value);
      else errs.push(r.error);
    }
    return [oks, errs];
  }
}

// Async utilities
export type AsyncResult<T, E> = Promise<Result<T, E>>;

export namespace AsyncResult {
  export async function fromPromise<T>(p: Promise<T>): AsyncResult<T, Error> {
    try { return Ok(await p); }
    catch (e) { return Err(e instanceof Error ? e : new Error(String(e))); }
  }

  export async function flatMap<T, U, E>(
    ar: AsyncResult<T, E>, fn: (v: T) => AsyncResult<U, E>
  ): AsyncResult<U, E> {
    const r = await ar;
    return r.ok ? fn(r.value) : r;
  }

  export async function map<T, U, E>(
    ar: AsyncResult<T, E>, fn: (v: T) => U | Promise<U>
  ): AsyncResult<U, E> {
    const r = await ar;
    return r.ok ? Ok(await fn(r.value)) : r;
  }
}

// Real-world usage
type ApiError =
  | { code: 'NOT_FOUND'; resource: string }
  | { code: 'VALIDATION'; fields: Record<string, string> }
  | { code: 'NETWORK'; message: string };

interface User { id: string; name: string; email: string; }

async function fetchUser(id: string): AsyncResult<User, ApiError> {
  try {
    const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
    if (res.status === 404) return Err({ code: 'NOT_FOUND', resource: `user:${id}` });
    if (!res.ok) return Err({ code: 'NETWORK', message: `HTTP ${res.status}` });
    return Ok(await res.json() as User);
  } catch (e) {
    return Err({ code: 'NETWORK', message: String(e) });
  }
}

async function fetchAllUsers(ids: string[]): AsyncResult<User[], ApiError> {
  const results = await Promise.all(ids.map(fetchUser));
  return Result.all(results);
}

async function main() {
  const result = await fetchUser('123');
  if (result.ok) {
    console.log(`Found: ${result.value.name}`);
  } else {
    switch (result.error.code) {
      case 'NOT_FOUND': console.log(`Missing: ${result.error.resource}`); break;
      case 'VALIDATION': console.log('Bad input:', result.error.fields); break;
      case 'NETWORK': console.log(`Network: ${result.error.message}`); break;
    }
  }
}
```

instruction: Explain the never type in TypeScript for exhaustive error handling and show how it catches missed cases at compile time.
output:
<think>
The `never` type in TypeScript represents values that never occur. It's the bottom type. Key uses for error handling:

1. Exhaustive switch checks: assign to never in default — if you miss a variant, TypeScript errors
2. Functions that never return: throw functions, infinite loops
3. Impossible states in discriminated unions
4. Assertion functions

The exhaustive check is the most important. Without it, adding a new variant to a union silently compiles. With the never check, the compiler catches it immediately.

I'll show: basic exhaustive checking, how it catches missing cases when you add a variant, assertNever helper, functions returning never, and impossible state detection.
</think>

```typescript
// assertNever: reusable exhaustive check helper
function assertNever(value: never, message?: string): never {
  throw new Error(message ?? `Unexpected value: ${JSON.stringify(value)}`);
}

// Exhaustive checking with discriminated union
type Shape =
  | { type: 'circle'; radius: number }
  | { type: 'rectangle'; width: number; height: number }
  | { type: 'triangle'; base: number; height: number };

function area(shape: Shape): number {
  switch (shape.type) {
    case 'circle': return Math.PI * shape.radius ** 2;
    case 'rectangle': return shape.width * shape.height;
    case 'triangle': return 0.5 * shape.base * shape.height;
    default: return assertNever(shape); // Compiles: all cases handled
  }
}

// Now add a variant — compiler catches the missing case:
type ShapeV2 = Shape | { type: 'pentagon'; sideLength: number };

function areaV2(shape: ShapeV2): number {
  switch (shape.type) {
    case 'circle': return Math.PI * shape.radius ** 2;
    case 'rectangle': return shape.width * shape.height;
    case 'triangle': return 0.5 * shape.base * shape.height;
    // MISSING: 'pentagon'!
    default:
      // COMPILE ERROR: Argument of type '{ type: "pentagon"; sideLength: number }'
      // is not assignable to parameter of type 'never'
      return assertNever(shape);
  }
}

// Error type exhaustive handling
type AppError =
  | { code: 'NOT_FOUND'; resource: string }
  | { code: 'UNAUTHORIZED'; reason: string }
  | { code: 'VALIDATION'; fields: Record<string, string> }
  | { code: 'RATE_LIMIT'; retryAfter: number };

function toHttpStatus(error: AppError): number {
  switch (error.code) {
    case 'NOT_FOUND': return 404;
    case 'UNAUTHORIZED': return 401;
    case 'VALIDATION': return 400;
    case 'RATE_LIMIT': return 429;
    default: return assertNever(error);
  }
}

// Functions that return never
function fail(message: string): never {
  throw new Error(message);
}

// TypeScript knows code after fail() is unreachable
function getRequiredEnv(key: string): string {
  const value = process.env[key];
  if (value === undefined) {
    fail(`Missing required env var: ${key}`);
    // No return needed — TS knows this is unreachable
  }
  return value; // TS narrows: value is string, not undefined
}

// Loading state with exhaustive render
type LoadingState<T> =
  | { status: 'idle' }
  | { status: 'loading' }
  | { status: 'success'; data: T }
  | { status: 'error'; error: Error };

function renderState<T>(state: LoadingState<T>): string {
  switch (state.status) {
    case 'idle': return 'Ready';
    case 'loading': return 'Loading...';
    case 'success': return `Data: ${JSON.stringify(state.data)}`;
    case 'error': return `Error: ${state.error.message}`;
    default: return assertNever(state);
  }
}

// The key insight: never forces you to handle every case.
// When you add a new variant to any union type, every switch
// statement using assertNever will produce a compile error
// until you add the new case. This is compile-time safety
// that prevents runtime "unhandled case" bugs.

export { assertNever, fail };
```
